speaker: interviewer
text: --Four with Paul Goldstein. And your mother survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As soon-- a few months after she had been the manicurist of the camp commander, or commandant, he was replaced by a commandant that was much more ferocious, much tougher on everybody. There was no way he was going to have a Jewess touch him. So she was00 he told her-- send her to Auschwitz on the transport. But the transport only went every 15th of the month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few days, my mother was just loafing around, if you can say that, waiting to go on the transport. And one day she stood near the laundry detail and there was a roll call for the laundry detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she knew-- she knew people there. And they call up the name of a lady, of a woman, an inmate, who was on the laundry detail. But she didn't respond that she was there. My mother jumped right smack into her spot. As a matter of fact, a friend that  she had kind of pulled her in there. And she says, here. And my mother then became part of the laundry detail. And no one's coming up for her. And that's how she, by luck and, and quick thinking-- but as I said, only a few hundred, I man, out of 28,000. But because she didn't have children with her, it was very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she take the name of that person for, that, that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This detail, I don't know. But what I do know is that all these people were interrogated. Where is your family? My mother used to tell me stories after the war. Where is your family? Do you have children? Et cetera, et cetera. Because they wanted to know, are they hiding?

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother really covered up very well. She says, my husband is, is deported. He must be in Auschwitz, you know? She didn't say that he was-- that he escaped. I mean, he was officially deported. So she said, I don't where my husband is. What about children? I don't have any children. How come you don't have any children? Because I, I couldn't bear children. I had a few miscarriages. I couldn't have children. And this is how she-- she-- all this happened. So-- Were there other stories she told you after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: From in the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there is one little episode which affects us directly, as to what happened during the war. And it has a certain interest in terms of this, of the subject that we're dealing with. I had mentioned that we had gone through this private school. And we kind of left it alone. But I want to get back to that for a moment. So this was the school, [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were there in the school, and there was-- happened to be another Jewish kid from the [NON-ENGLISH] also in that school. Unfortunately he wasn't as reticent or as secretive as we were, and he blabbered a little bit to the other kids. And slowly it came out, because of him, that kids started to think that my brother and I would be Jewish, because of that other little boy. And before we knew it, here we are being beaten up. I've got to give you-- some kids used to gang up on us. I remember the kid, a very anti-Semitic kid that was going to that school. His name-- first name was Basil. I don't remember his last name. And then him and a few others used to get brass knuckles and surround my brother and I and just beat us up and call us dirty Jew. It would have been only a matter of time before we would have been turned in, and before that whole school and the principal would have-- I didn't realize-- again, we had no defense. But it became tough. It became difficult. And what happened-- call it fate, call it  a miracle, call it whatever you want to call it. We were in that school on April the 5th, 1943. So a few months later. The Americans decided that they were going to wipe out that [NON-ENGLISH] plant, which was manufacturing parts for the Air Force, for the German Air Force, and where Mr. Roggeman worked. Now, I'm trying to tell this from the perspective that I was in, and then I'll explain from what I found out later. So we're in class. I'm in grade four or five, the grade I would have been in if I'd, if I'd still gone to school in [NON-ENGLISH]. And my brother was in grade one. And my class-- it was quite a building, and it had a  few stories. There was a courtyard in the middle, and I was something in the back. And my brother was in the classroom in the front-- at the front entrance on the street. And we are in a Flemish class. Flemish was one of the languages. As a matter of fact, the name of the Flemish teacher was Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] Don't ask me how I remember that. And all of a sudden we hear shooting. Says, Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH], they're shooting. Oh, she said, they're always shooting. There's always exercises from war. Three minutes later, our walls cave in. The classroom is full of dust. Bombs are falling on the school, and the panic sets in. We all rush out to catch air. We're  almost suffocating. I'm alone. My brother wasn't in the same classroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a total panic. We force ourselves to the door. It's up in the courtyard. A rain of glass, like hail from all the windows, on our heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- like cattle, we follow the, the group towards the garden in the back. All of a sudden, a big bang. I turned around, and where I had stood-- where I was one second before, a humongous bomb hit and  a humongous hole, just behind me, like, one second later. We, we all run to the back, and we hit this fence. We can't go any further. Everybody is hysterical and sobbing, not knowing-- we're in a panic. Like, in-- we've been bombarded and we're going to get killed. One little girl in the class-- I should make an attempt once to see if  she if she's still alive. Her name was Paula [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember that. And she looks around and she says, whoever can pray, pray. And we all started to pray. Everybody calmed down. All of a sudden I realized that  I'm bleeding from the chest, just full of blood. I-- it was just-- it was just-- all of a sudden, Mrs. [INAUDIBLE], the principal appears. And she calms us down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything is burning around us. At that moment, I realize that I have a little brother in there. I completely forgot about my brother in grade one. I say, where's Rene? Where's Rene? So I start looking for him. So I get to the front of the building where his class was, and there's this black shirt SS-- SS man. The Belgian SS were in black. And he sees. He's looking at it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he calls me over to talk to me. As soon as he did that, I turned around and I just disappeared. I vanished. There was no way I was going to talk to an SS, no matter what. And my brother showed up about an hour later. He, with another kid, had run to the front of the building. And the other kid wanted to cross the street. My brother didn't want to cross the street. Instinct-- stayed there. And as he crossed the street, my brother-- little kid-- a bomb hit and killed that little kid in front of my brother's eyes. Across the street was a French Catholic private school, Institute [INAUDIBLE]. All the kids were in the basement sheltered and they all suffocated. They all died. Just across the street. The next time I saw my brother he was on the knees of the dentist or doctor, from me to him. He was a little child. He, he was [INAUDIBLE] to see that little boy that got killed. What had happened was that the tactic of the American Air Force was that when it went after the target, they would send out a reconnaissance plane that would then draw a circle of smoke over the target. And then those who dropped their bombs in there was supposed to follow the target. But, but between being chased by Messerschmitts and, and not being able to communicate with other troops, he drew in a circle, instead of over the factory, right over the heart of the town, of the town. We were exactly in the focal point of this bombardment. 1,500 people died just within a few hundred yards of where we were. Every year on April the 5th, there's a big commemoration with a, with a, with a big parade to commemorate that. As matter of fact, that-- my blood was from a shrapnel that glanced off  my sternum. And you see here, the white here, and the white spot. That flesh, there's no--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's scar tissue.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's scar tissue. From the-- I was lucky. Otherwise, I would have been killed. The point was, and then the Roggeman's showed up. And then between the corpse and the bodies, they took us back. That was the end of the school. The school was totally flattened. And there was no way that we would ever-- that was the end of us going to school. They could chance-- And in thinking back, I think that's what saved us, because we got so close to being found out because of this other kid talking that we were Jewish that we  started getting beaten up as Jews in that private school. If it hadn't been for this thing happening, god knows if we wouldn't have been caught and denounced by these kids as-- so this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if that other child, the one who blabbed, survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He did survive. Also, I don't know what and how-- I accidentally-- because I left Belgium very shortly after the war. I've been there since 1954. But survived-- I think at one point in time many years ago, I even spoke to him. But there was no way that I could go back and, and torment him with the fact as to what he had done when he was eight or nine years old. I mean, there was simply-- he wouldn't acknowledge it, or remember it, or whatever. I didn't probably do it willfully either. He wasn't out to denounce us but he-- he-- so that, that was the-- my, my schooling during the war. [LAUGHS] An extra little side episode. And my mother, who-- by the way, Christian friends who were visiting in this camp where my mother was and they used to smuggle letters in and out also. And when she heard or whatever, knew-- she heard that this-- she knew that I went to that school. That the school was bombed-- she almost went crazy when she thought if I still alive. Anyway, that's-- so that also helped us to stay alive because we got very close-- as close as we got, to getting caught because we were found out to being Jewish in that school just before the bombardment. Interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh huh. OK. So we are now going to this-- we are now in the fall-- first of all, June of 1944, I hear on the BBC, in the cupboard, between the bomb, bomb, bomb, bombs, scrambling, that there was an invasion in Normandy. I hear this. The next day, I hear there was an attempt on Hitler. [NON-ENGLISH] All this I, I, I knew. Never talked about it. Never said a word. Roggeman's didn't even know that I knew they had  a radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk with Rene about these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rene was three and 1/2 years younger. He was like a baby. Rene was only six-years-old, six and a half. But I did talk with neighbors. Something very ironic happened, which makes this, this little episode so, so paradoxical. Among our neighbors, we had-- in that population-- especially among the Flemish population-- you had anti-Germans and pro-Germans, collaborators and un-collaborators. It was very difficult to know who was whom. For instance, at the end of the street lived this lady. She had a little, her little flower garden. And her son was a, was a volunteer for the German SS. And after one week on the Eastern Front-- he  got killed on the Eastern Front in Russia. What we thought was a friendly policeman in the town was a collaborator. What we thought was a, was a, was, was, was, was a stern person, a dangerous person, was part of the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our neighbors, some of them started to resent us. Why? Because they thought that we were collaborators because they saw these saw this German officer coming in and out of the house all the time. And we didn't realize that this was a kind of cover for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't realize this at the time. And we, we were kind of resented. Some of the kids stopped playing with us because they thought that we were-- others looked upon us as friendly. I mean, it was so mixed up, you know, that it wasn't even funny. We didn't which way was up. And the ones that were, that were anti-German kind of thought that we were-- one day-- this is something that I've just got to throw in because there were moments of such panic in this whole thing. Panic on top of panic.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- as I said, I was pretty good at, at not engaging in conversations. As a matter of fact, one day when we went to the school in '43, I ran into a couple. Hi, Paul and Rene! How are you? Well, where, where are your parents? These were clients from the beauty parlor that recognized us. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. We can't talk. We  don't have time to talk. I grabbed my brother and vanished. Never even started a conversation. Somehow I was pretty good at  that. [LAUGHS] I didn't even give them like, the time of day-- further the chance to know. So one day, we were sitting-- incidentally, as I said, the trains were passing. And this was like seeing the war passing in front of us. Because when American planes used to go overhead towards Germany to bomb in formations. And the planes used to stop and the activity on the, on the train used to start firing at the planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So like, I'm watching through the window here. I see those real, real trains with real fire shoot over our rooftop. And one day they shot down a plane over-- one of the planes that came down about a  kilometer from where we were. It was-- it was Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, the Germans used to shoot at the-- at the, at the people jump by parachute. When they shot down a plane, and, and, and the parachuter jumped down, they used to shoot at the parachuters as well. We saw all of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, we're sitting there in the front room. And there's a delegation of Gestapo, high-ranking German military SS and Gestapo coming to our door. In an instant we thought this was it. They came to arrest us. What business did they have to come to our door?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember the sinking feeling of knowing that this is it, that they're coming now to arrest you. That was-- I know how that feels also. And so did the Roggemans. Because they also-- in spite of everything, they were hiding Jewish kids. Whatever the motivation, whatever the treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went to the door to open the door. We were kind of ready to be taken away. And she comes back and says, don't worry. All they came for was-- as I told you, it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And the next door, in a-- couple of houses away, there was this big piece of land. And they were planning to make headquarters for the German government forces, occupying forces.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just came out for information to know what she knew about-- what she knew about that place, et cetera, et cetera. Stuff like that. But we thought they'd be-- that was the end of it. Moments like that. This was like, typical of, of living in a state of panic. Panic all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked to the neighbors. You said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Like I-- that guy was the-- Next door, they had a little plot of-- what do you call when someone-- you have this here too-- when you just rent a garden?

speaker: interviewer
text: A tenant farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A tenant farmer. I forgot that expression. A tenant farmer next door-- very nice guy. And him and I used to talk politics, about what's going on in the front and all of that. Somehow he seemed to be on the, on the, on the anti-German side. And him and I, we used to talk like as if I was a-- some kind of an expert on, on what goes on in the war, because I was so aware of what was going on. What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. I don't remember his name. I don't remember his name. The-- I remember the shops where he used to go and buy the fruit and the vegetables. I remember the bakery. Right before the baker had a chance to come in, I used to always steal a piece of yeast from the jar.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to know-- you know, it was a dream to have a piece of chocolate. So once in a while, I had to go buy a piece of chocolate for the Roggemans. We never, ever had any of it. Never had any of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the, the idea was not to give yourself away. So who we talked to, how much we would talk. And then again, we had to play kind of that we were normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I think that most of the regular school hours, thinking back, we were supposed to stay in. So we were only released on non-school hours so people wouldn't wonder what are these kids doing out of school. And I always was an avid reader, but all I read was that-- all I remember was that Mrs. Roggeman, there was a newspaper called the [NON-ENGLISH] And it had this serial story that appeared like, once a week-- novels, serial. And then she-- [FRENCH], they say in French. And in Flemish, this was. And she used to cut them out. They were adult stories and all that. And she used to bundle together and put them in the shed where the wood was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whenever I used to, to bring in the wood, I used to hide there for a few minutes and used to, to, to to read, you see? Yes. It was-- So, '44, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Canadians-- there were rumblings that the Allies are approaching. And we started seeing the German trains going all north, towards Holland, full with their supplies and so forth. And one day, one day, there was little bridge that went  over the track. And we were about half a mile away from the main highway and towards Brussels. And, and we hear people rushing towards the highway. This was the fall of 1944, late fall. Rushing towards the highway. The, the Allies are on the highway. So I-- I, I'm outside, and I'm running to this highway as well. Until this day, I remember that site. Three Canadian tanks, Canadian tanks, on the highway going north. And the people, local people, all cheering and, and, and throwing flowers. And in front of each turret there was a German prisoner with his hands up in the air like this. And the chap in the turret, the driver, used to have a gun in the guy's back. Only many, many years later, when I was active in the local community and, and, and organized all kinds of celebration of the liberation, and I met the chiefs of staff of the greater Armed Forces-- I remember the Major General Fleury was the head of the Quebec command. And I told them this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, there was a reason. Why? Because they had these Germans up there to protect the driver from German snipers. I didn't realize that at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those-- the Canadians were absolutely reckless. And not-- they were-- those three tanks were like, about half an hour ahead-- there was little resistance on that highway-- of the other troops. And I heard-- found out later that when they reached a, a place called the Albert Canal, which is a canal that runs past Antwerp. When they went over the bridge, the Germans lay in waiting. And as soon as they went over the bridge, the Germans bombed the bridge. And they-- they were destroyed. They didn't make it past. So they were the Canadians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I went back home, I was so happy with this liberation that I threw my-- I had a cap-- my cap in the air. And unfortunately, if fell over the bridge onto the track. And I was afraid to go home because I lost my cap on the day of liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] This I remember. [LAUGHS] The psychology of that. So we're liberated, but nothing happens. And a week goes by, two weeks go by. The Roggemans say, well, your parents are gone, and we have no more use for you. And we'll start looking for an orphanage to  put you in. They had no interest in keeping us. So that was-- we were gonna-- supposed to be in an orphanage. That was going to be our fate after the war. About two and and a half weeks after this, she started to inquire about orphanages. And I'm standing in front of our little bedroom window, and all of a sudden I see my father and mother in front of the house. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I can tell you that I-- if there was an Olympic record for, for, for running down the stairs, I must have broken it then. [LAUGHS] The reason it took so long was that there was no transportation, plus chaos. Troops went through. The camp where my mother was liberated. My father was-- came out of hiding. He managed to survive in that same place where  my mother was in. My mother's camp was liberated. The German's abandoned it the day before. So everybody was let loose. My father was on foot. We're talking here about 20 miles maybe, on foot, went to that camp, found my mother. Then they found-- and then somehow on foot, they came-- they came to us. And that was the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened was that the, the elation of this moment, between being liberated and having my parents back against all the odds-- it must have been very-- there were very, very few families that were fortunate enough to have both parents and both children, because my uncle's and aunts weren't all that fortunate. My uncle Jacques never came back from Auschwitz. He didn't jump from the train. His wife, Rochelle or Rachel, and her little boy-- they had a little daughter that was born in 1940. So she  was only two years old when all this started. And they managed to put her in hiding with an elderly couple. But the wife was caught with the, with the child. My aunt Rosa was caught with the two children. My mother went to give her food. All the-- they never came back. They're all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the little girl was Rosette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosette. Unfortunately, the people that hid her-- so my father found her, through-- because now the community organized to locate people-- try and find kids in that were in hiding, that were baptized, kids that-- tried to unite family members from all over the place. Few people had survived. And so we found Rosette. Now, she had the same name as my father, so my father right away made arrangements to take Rosette in. And as far as she was concerned, she was very weak. She was absolutely a physical wreck. Unfortunate-- I hate to say this-- but those older people turned out to be a pair of perverts. And she was sleeping with them in their own beds and what they did with her-- this kid was absolutely a psychological and physical wreck. She couldn't even walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: For years, I carried her on my shoulders And she-- and then, then gradually, she recovered and we looked after her. And one day, unfortunately, when she was about 10 or 11, a local constable comes in. And he says that for the-- for the registration purposes, are the family members in this house so and so, and so and so, and so and so? Then he says, is Rosette Goldstein here? Daughter of Jacques, deceased. And she hears that from him, and she got-- went into a state of shock, because until then she thought that my parents were her parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had never forgiven my parents for not telling her the truth about her own parents. And then she couldn't forgive the fact that as a child, that my parents were alive and hers weren't. She couldn't understand that. And that became an absolutely-- war between her and my, my mother. It's only-- it also wrecked her own life. She was never able to put her life together. It's only a year and a half ago, or a couple of years, that my mother was-- was going downhill in the senior home in Belgium that they finally reconciled. And after all these years. That's, that's Rosette.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take another short break.
